title: Treat every feature as a thinking problem first: spec-driven AI development
whatItDoes: A five-stage loop for building with AI agents: rubber-duck the problem with the model, write a short PRD, break it into issues, let the agent implement, then review with AI-assisted audits including mutation testing to verify test coverage.
whatItImproves: Moves cognitive effort upstream — problems get caught at the spec stage instead of through trial-and-error coding, and the audit stage catches weak tests the implementation produced.
howToTry: 1. Start a conversation with your agent to frame and validate the problem before any code. 2. Have it write a short PRD and break it into issues. 3. Implement issue by issue. 4. Finish with an AI review pass and mutation testing on the new tests.

title: Let the model pick a cheaper sub-model for grunt work
whatItDoes: Instead of prescribing exactly how the agent should work, add a standing instruction telling it to use its own judgement to choose an appropriate lower-power model and run that work in a subagent. Judgement-heavy reasoning stays in the main loop while routine implementation is delegated down.
whatItImproves: Cuts token spend noticeably without a visible drop in quality or velocity — Simon Willison reported getting a ton of work done while his Claude allowance shrank far more slowly than before.
howToTry: 1. Open your Claude Code memory / CLAUDE.md. 2. Add a line like: 'For coding tasks, use your judgement to decide an appropriate lower-power model and run that in a subagent.' 3. Let the agent decide per-task which model fits. 4. Watch usage and correct the guidance if it under- or over-delegates.
author: Simon Willison

title: Run Claude Code as a daily driver with CLAUDE.md, skills, subagents and MCPs
whatItDoes: A walkthrough of using Claude Code as an everyday development tool by layering its advanced features: a project CLAUDE.md for standing context, reusable skills, task-scoped subagents, plugins, and MCP servers that connect the agent to external systems.
whatItImproves: Turns ad-hoc prompting into a repeatable setup — shared project context and reusable skills cut ramp-up on each task and keep the agent consistent across a team.
howToTry: 1. Add a CLAUDE.md at your repo root with project conventions. 2. Capture recurring workflows as skills. 3. Delegate scoped work to subagents. 4. Wire in an MCP server for the external tools you use most.
author: arps18

title: Ask for an HTML artifact instead of Markdown for rich explanations
whatItDoes: When you want an explanation, review, or report, prompt the model to produce a self-contained HTML artifact rather than Markdown. HTML lets it add SVG diagrams, color-coded severity, inline margin annotations, and in-page navigation that plain text can't express.
whatItImproves: Complex output becomes far more navigable and scannable — a PR review or technical explainer with diagrams and severity coloring reads in a fraction of the time versus a wall of Markdown.
howToTry: 1. Feed the model your content (e.g. a diff or doc). 2. Explicitly ask for an HTML artifact, not Markdown. 3. Name the interactive elements you want: tables, color-coded findings, in-page anchors. 4. Ask for semantic CSS styling for clarity.
author: Simon Willison (via Thariq Shihipar)

title: Build AI evals by reading your traces, not guessing
whatItDoes: A grounded four-step method for building trustworthy evals for an LLM app: read a sample of real production traces, write freeform 'open coding' notes on whatever went wrong, cluster those notes into a handful of recurring failure modes, then build a targeted eval — often an LLM-as-judge — for the most common ones. Demonstrated live on real production data from Nurture Boss.
whatItImproves: Replaces vibes-based 'looks good to me' checking with evals grounded in your own failures, so you measure the few things that actually break instead of chasing generic benchmark scores.
howToTry: 1. Pull 30-100 real traces from your app. 2. Open-code each one: write a short freeform note on any problem, with no predefined categories. 3. Axial-code: cluster those notes into a small set of named failure modes. 4. Build an eval (a code check or an LLM-as-judge) for the top failure modes, and validate the judge against your own human labels before trusting it.
author: Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar (AI Engineer)

title: Let the agent grill you with questions before it writes any code
whatItDoes: The 'Grill Me' skill flips planning around: instead of the agent jumping to code from a short brief, it interrogates you with 20-80 questions about the feature — surfacing design branches, edge cases, and hidden assumptions — and the resulting dialogue becomes the design concept you build from.
whatItImproves: Misalignment and missing requirements get caught during planning instead of after implementation, and because you answer the questions yourself you stay connected to the codebase rather than losing the thread in a specs-to-code handoff.
howToTry: 1. Install or recreate a 'grill me' skill that instructs the agent to ask questions instead of writing code. 2. Paste your rough brief (e.g. a Slack message) and let it interrogate you. 3. Answer the questions together until the plan is aligned. 4. Turn the conversation into a PRD and break it into thin vertical slices before letting the agent implement.
author: Matt Pocock
